claimed_contribution: Introduces customization via starting solutions (CvSS), a two-stage customization architecture in which consumers first choose a prespecified product and then freely refine its attributes, as a remedy for the customization paradox.
Provides evidence from nine studies, including three field settings, that CvSS improves outcomes for both consumers and firms relative to standard attribute-by-attribute customization.

Identifies and tests two psychological mechanisms underlying CvSS effects - reduced perceived process complexity and enhanced mental simulation of product use - and examines how the number of starting solutions affects effectiveness.
context: The paper studies online product and service configurators where consumers customize offerings. Empirical contexts include shirts, automobiles, vacation packages, jewelry, and financial investment portfolios.
design_and_data: The paper reports nine studies comparing CvSS with standard attribute-by-attribute customization. Three studies use field data or field experiments: shirts (transaction/process data for 842 purchases on a manufacturer website), automobiles (random assignment on a manufacturer website; 160 buyers who ordered), and vacation packages (random assignment on a resort website; 323 starters, analyses on 194 buyers). Five controlled online experiments test mediating mechanisms and a boundary condition in jewelry, investment portfolios, and automobiles (sample sizes from 100 to 336), and a final online study (n = 100) measures architecture preference across 36 product domains. Measures include feature richness, price, completion time, perceived process complexity, mental simulation of product use, and choice satisfaction.
main_findings: Across studies, CvSS led consumers to configure more feature-rich products and often pay higher prices than attribute-by-attribute customization.
CvSS increased satisfaction with customized products, and this effect was explained by lower perceived process complexity and higher mental simulation of product use.

CvSS benefits were robust across multiple domains but weakened when too many starting solutions were shown; in the automobile test, effects began to taper when the number of starting solutions exceeded about 8 to 16.
one_line_summary: A two-stage customization interface that starts from prespecified solutions and then allows free refinement outperforms standard attribute-by-attribute customization by lowering perceived complexity, increasing mental simulation, and improving both satisfaction and revenue-related choice outcomes.
relation_to_prior_work: extends product customization research by integrating a choice-architecture approach that contrasts with the dominant attribute-by-attribute format. It refines distinctions from defaults and additive/subtractive option framing by emphasizing that CvSS uses multiple consumer-chosen starting solutions with bidirectional modification. It also extends mental simulation work by showing that architecture-induced top-down processing during customization changes both evaluations and chosen feature richness.
research_gap: Prior research documents a customization paradox: attribute-by-attribute customization can increase decision complexity and reduce net satisfaction despite offering preference fit. The paper argues that the literature lacked a customization architecture that lowers process burden without reducing consumers' freedom to configure any attribute combination.
research_question: Can product customization be redesigned so consumers retain full configurational freedom but experience less decision burden than under standard attribute-by-attribute interfaces? Specifically, does a two-stage CvSS format change perceived complexity, mental simulation, feature richness of chosen products, and choice satisfaction, and are these effects mediated by complexity reduction and mental simulation?
